title: Kaitiakitanga in Action: Protecting Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Te Mahi Kaitiaki: Te Tiaki i Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: This lesson is grounded in the whakapapa of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. According to Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau, the stream was named for the kōtukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once shaded its banks, providing habitat for native birds and medicinal resources for our tūpuna. Students will explore how the health of this awa directly impacts the physical, mental, and spiritual wellbeing of our community.
learningObjectives: Conduct a water quality assessment of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using scientific tools and mātauranga Māori observation methods
Analyze how changes in the awa's health impact community wellbeing through interviews with local kaumātua and health professionals
Develop and present a kaitiakitanga action plan that addresses an identified environmental health issue affecting the awa
Reflect on personal connection to place and responsibility as kaitiaki through written and visual journaling

activities.title: Whakapapa of the Awa
activities.description: Begin at the streamside with a karakia and welcome from a local kaumātua who will share the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students create visual maps showing historical and current connections between the awa and community health, identifying traditional food gathering sites, swimming holes, and medicinal plant areas.

activities.title: Mauri Meter Investigation
activities.description: Using water testing kits and traditional observation methods (clarity, odor, presence of insects/birds), students assess the mauri (life force) of three different sites along the awa. They record physical measurements (pH, nitrates, turbidity) alongside qualitative observations of cultural health indicators.

activities.title: Community Voice Circle
activities.description: Students interview local fishermen, farmers, health workers, and kaumātua about changes they've observed in the awa and how these changes affect community wellbeing. Recorded interviews are analyzed for connections between environmental and human health.

activities.title: Kaitiakitanga Action Planning
activities.description: In small groups, students develop practical action plans addressing one identified issue (e.g., riparian planting to reduce runoff, educational signage, community clean-up event). Plans must incorporate both scientific and mātauranga Māori approaches to healing the awa.

activities.title: Pepeha Presentation
activities.description: Students create and present an updated pepeha that includes their role as kaitiaki of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, sharing their action plans and personal commitments to the health of the awa and community.

resources: Hawke's Bay Regional Council water testing kits and monitoring guidelines
Local kaumātua Hone Smith (Ngāti Kahungunu) for pūrākau and historical knowledge
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) resources
Book: 'Te Mātauranga o te Wai' by Māori Water Research Centre

tasks: Water quality investigation report combining scientific data and cultural observations
Recorded interview analysis showing connections between environmental and community health
Kaitiakitanga action plan presentation to community stakeholders
Reflective journal entry on personal growth as kaitiaki

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A3 - Safety management; B1 - Personal identity; C2 - Community resources; D1 - Societal attitudes
Science: Nature of Science - Investigating in science; Living World - Ecology
Social Studies: Continuity and Change; Place and Environment
